title: Expertise Matters More Than Ever | The Aboard Podcast 2026-03-17

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Hi, I'm Paul Ford.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And I'm Rich Ziade.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And this is the Aboard podcast, the podcast about how AI is changing the world of software. Richard, how are you?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I'm doing well. How are you?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I'm good. Let's play the theme song and let's get into it.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Let's get to work!

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I want to talk about, get ready, this is gonna be pretty exciting: pricing.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Oof.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: First of all, I want to actually just help everyone who has people from the Middle East in their life.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Because a lot of people are confused about the etiquette of what to say to the person from the Middle East with family in the Middle East in your life during yet another global crisis.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And I'm going to just give everyone this advice. You go, here, we'll work, we'll do it. Ready?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: How's your family?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: You wanna make an egg and cheese as you walk into my bodega?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: That's about right. How, like that's about it. Like everybody, because Americans are like, 'I'd like to explore your trauma.' No, it's 'How's your family?' Everybody's okay? Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: That's it. Nobody wants anything else.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Anything else other than this Arizona iced tea?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Nobody wants anything else. Uh, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: There's a, I can't speak for, like, there's many countries in the Middle East, but the Lebanese are, don't wear pity well. They don't want you saying, 'Are you okay?' They just, they don't like it.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: They do, people, people like a 'How's your family?'

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: How's your family? Yeah. Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: That's it. And then what they'll do is they'll divert the conversation.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: They don't want 'How are you?' Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I don't know when this airs. The world may have moved on.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Oh, I'm sure it's all gonna be fixed up by the time—

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It is early March right now and the world's a little on fire. So say hi to everyone.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah. That's it. This, but that's it, then on to the next thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yes. So that's what we're doing right now. Your family's good.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And I'm going to tell you a story, and it's a true story about projects coming into our organization. Because we have a platform that we build on, but the way that AI works is you actually need to kind of help and advise organizations too.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay, so that's what we do. You come to us and we're like, 'Hey, I can build that for you, sometimes in like a minute.'

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Anyone who's selling you shrink-wrapped boxes of AI, be wary is what I would say.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah. So someone came in, I would say this is like four, five months ago. Uh-huh. And somebody I knew, and I won't give, it's a client project, so I'm not going to like, or customer project, depending on how the weather is on a given day. I like to use the term 'clustomer' for the—

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: For the—

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: For the client in the age of AI, where you can deliver so much so quickly.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Mailchimp apparently has some ad campaign where they're like 'cluster of customers' and they kind of amass everyone in—

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It sounds really clever.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah. I'm saying it's a combination of client and customer, and if we're gonna come up with really—

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: terrible words, I want to claim it back.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Take it! I mean if you're going to claim anything, you might as well grab that.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I'm working on neologisms that explain the moment. So part- that's one of them. The other one I've got, by the way, is- is 'illustrator', but it's spelled T-R-A-I-T-O-R.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Oh!

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, it's someone who uses AI instead of- instead of actually doing it by hand.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay, you're running with this, I can see.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I gotta have some talks to give, you know? Anyway, regardless. So they come in and they go, 'Hey, I got this data and I really want to make it accessible and usable.'

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And it was like in PDF and spreadsheets and all over.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Littered everywhere in different formats.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, and I was like, 'Alright, well let me get you a proposal for that.' But I was like, 'Give me the data.'

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: They didn't commit as a customer. No, no, no. You just said, 'Give me the data'. Maybe there was an NDA, I don't know anything about the background.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No. That's why I'm not giving any details.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay, so then, okay they gave you- they gave you the data? What was the- was it a zip file?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Big, like thousand page PDF, lots of- lots of stuff in it. Okay, and lots of ecological data, things like that. And then a whole bunch of spreadsheets around locations.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay. Pile of stuff.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So here's five years ago it would have been like, 'Alright, let's go take a look at this and then we'll describe how we're going to convert it and what a process might look like-'

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Maybe write a proposal.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And the goal here is to make a product that people use, that people can use to explore the data, they maybe you could, you know, do some newsletters on top of it. Like they want to make it active. Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And so, you know, as- as I'm writing that proposal five years ago, the numbers are going up: 10,000, 20,000, 30,000, 40,000. Like it's expensive because you need a team of engineers and you need a process.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Takes time. Data- just scaffolding and setting up the database takes time.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Sure. So I went home and I vibe-coded a little bit. And actually in two hours I had a pretty good data explorer going. The PDF was all turned into images. I'd used Claude Vision. I spent about $15 of CPU time and several hours of my time.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay. So I threw it over the wall.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I put it on a website. I was like, 'You know, I was going to send you a proposal-'

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It became a website.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I was like, 'I was going to send you a proposal, but here's the data if you want to explore it. We can use this to guide the next conversation.' Yep. Two weeks go by. Silence.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Yeah, yeah, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And this is- I was expecting this because these are not- like if I send you a proposal you're going to go, 'Oh, received it. We're going to talk about it and I'll get back in touch.'

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: But I sent you a data product that you didn't ask for.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So person comes back. Two weeks have gone by. They're like 'This is truly interesting, okay, I kind of get my head around it', ropes some other folks in, we have another conversation. We talk about the kind of app they want to build, how you could really use this data. Okay. Same thing. I go, I'm about to like turn in a proposal, I'm like, 'Well wait, let's see how far we can go.' Get the whole app as a mobile app.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: You kept going.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Well I was like, why not?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Why'd you keep going?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Because have you- you've written proposals. How would you describe that process?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It's terrible. It's boring as shit. Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You're playing around- well this is- this is the other thing. You're...

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: you enjoy building stuff.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: This is also a person I like. I want to like have a relationship here.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Right? I want I want--

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Not an intimate one.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: No, no, no. That's not--

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Personal relationship. Professional relationship. Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: A professional relationship. Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay. All right. Cause I've seen different ways to get there.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, well, now it's really complicated. But yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay. So you kept going and what you made another version of it?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I made... well, now I built the whole app with the onboarding. And it did a really good job.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Like, like really good. Like, like I would have put a junior designer on this prototype and gotten this result.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So let me throw this back at you for a second. Wait.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: So then I throw this over. Two weeks go by. We have another call and they're like, I guess who want to move forward.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: But we don't even know what that means.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And and then they're like, and what would it cost?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And I had to just go like, I don't have the first freaking clue, man.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Like, I'm trying to be transparent. I'm trying to drive this value towards people. I know that I bring a lot of value because very few people can currently unlock like a product-level delivery right now. So I know there's that.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: But I also don't buy this thing where it's like, well, they should just pay you for your value, which could be $10,000 a minute. Like, that's that's not how the world works, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: So I'm throwing this back to you to do real-time strategy because how do you price these things now? Where is the value? How is this going to look? Because this would have been $400,000 four years ago.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay, before I go into real-time strategy mode...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: What was your favorite real-time strategy game?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Command & Conquer.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Okay. And then Command & Conquer Red Alert.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Thank you.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: We don't need to go there. That's a separate podcast.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I know. The audience would far prefer if we went there.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I know. They're wonderful games. There are great current real-time strategy games today. Total Annihilation and then Planetary Annihilation, which came out a few years ago but has like a rabid following. And then Starcraft.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: But wait, so for people who are not as cool as you are, what is real-time strategy?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It is a video game where you don't need eye-hand coordination, but it's not turn-based. So time is passing, and so you do need to think on your feet as time goes by. And it's really fun and they're they're just--

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: So wait, compare this to like SimCity. Like, SimCity time is passing, things happen.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: SimCity is similar except SimCity is world building and so you're not battling an enemy.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Ah, okay. So when you say real-time strategy, there's like a war going on.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: There's a war going on. By the way, I mean, shout out to South Korea, where Starcraft is like the equivalent of chess in South Korea. There are like cafes where to this day--

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: No, big stadiums.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Stadiums get filled up with Starcraft, which is a legendary real-time strategy game.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: We should actually... there's a brief digression it's worth while here. There... what was it? It's Command & Conquer.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Don't be a dad. Yes, it's Command & Conquer and Command & Conquer Red Alert.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I just never got into RTS.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Oh, you never did?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: No. It's just not my thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Oh, so good.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: That's the one with Tim Curry.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Why would you focus on that?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Because it's an excellent--

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: The game mechanics are phenomenal.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: No, this is like the only thing I know.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yes, Tim Curry is the enemy.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: This is the only thing I know is like he just had this one day where he recorded and he's just like, 'I'm going to the only place where you can be a communist: space!' or something like that.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yes, he is a communist actually in it. And he's excellent. And he wasn't recognized for his performance.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: So the—no, he was recognized. The video is legendary. We'll put a clip. It's some of the most ridiculous video game nonsense that has ever existed in history. It's wonderful.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yes, yes. Also George Takei is in it.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: That makes sense. Like when the—the Star Trek guys doing video games in the late '80s on CD-ROM makes sense to me.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It—it's all like pretty ugly racist stereotyping, frankly, but for some reason like the rules didn't show up to video games until later.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Oh, but when they did.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So George Takei's in it, Malcolm McDowell's in it, who's like Europe.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Speaking—oh really? So wait, so Tim Curry is—

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Tim Curry is like Soviet or Eastern Bloc or—

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: George Takei represents Asia in the game.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Let's—yes. It was a bad time. But it's—it's worth checking. There are—I'm sure the clips are on YouTube. We'll share whatever we can share. It's hilarious. But—there's like an oral history of the Tim Curry performance.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Is there really?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah. This is what I know about. I never played the game. Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: The game is excellent. Check out the game. The game is still sold on Steam to this day for like 10 bucks because it's so good.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Maybe we—maybe instead of continually running this company, we could take a little break and get Command & Conquer going?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I set up a LAN party when I was young. It was Quake I and the neighborhood showed up.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Really? So it wasn't just you?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No, that's the point of a LAN party.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Well, I know, but I could see you setting up a LAN party for myself. Yeah. All right.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: But I digress. I want to make two points based on your little story here.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: My wittle story.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No, it's not little. It's interesting. Thanks. The first is acknowledging just how exploding the mind this all is. This is the equivalent of me calling a restaurant, saying, 'Hey, we saw your website, we might want to book a reservation, but we're not sure yet. Are you guys open Saturday?' And they're like, 'Yeah, we're open Saturday.' 'Okay, great. We'll call you back.' And then an hour later, the restaurant's van shows up, rolls out a leg of lamb on a spit and says, 'Well, listen, I don't know if you're booking this Saturday, but if you want to try the lamb, it's here.'

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It's exactly like that and it—I don't think necessarily a good feeling on the other side.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It's too much, right? And you would think that 'Wow, they—they should just lean into this.' What the brain does is it goes to 'I cannot bet my business or my organization on crazy stuff like this.' That's where their head goes. They don't go to 'Wow, let's do this,' right?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Which leads me to my second point before I get into real-time strategy mode. My second point is you and we are building a culture and building tools to actually ship production-grade stuff. Everyone else is hearing about how AI is moving too fast and how you can't trust the software. And the truth is we actually are doing that deep thinking around shipping something legitimate. By the way, I sell, I've sold a lot in my life. If you're having to explain why everything is going to be okay, the sale is not going well.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, that's a good point.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It's not going well. That means you're trying to calm them down, you're trying to get some relax. To be clear, this is someone who I knew so I'm ready to take a little more of a flyer.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: We don't do this with everyone. That's a huge point.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I wouldn't. You're right. It's too confusing.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It's too confusing.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You know what it is? It's as you were talking, I'm just like, we've all known a lot of dudes. Everyone who listened to this podcast has known a lot of dudes. And when a dude, you know what dudes talk about? They talk about like, boy she's really hot, but she's pretty crazy. That's the status of AI software right now. Everybody's like, wow that's really hot. Yeah. Should I roll the dice here? Yeah. But why she just like cut her face with a little knife when we were out at the restaurant? I appreciate this analogy because it typically comes from me and you're usually calming me down. No because this is, well the thing is when a dude tells you she's hot and crazy, you know you're about to lose him for six months and he's going to come back either a lot thinner or a lot fatter and he's going to like you're going to get a call six months from now and he's going to go like, 'Hey man I don't know you want to go like just go out to a movie or something?' and you're going to be like 'Yeah because that's what.' That's what AI's going to do to everybody.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It's doing it. I mean, we're here and we're focusing on software.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: The you see middle-aged guys, it's the middle-aged guy with the crazy younger girlfriend and then suddenly they're getting into shape and then they dye their hair like a weird color.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It's just too black. Yeah, and then the sweater gets tight.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: That's what AI is doing to our industry. Yeah, it's doing it to many industries, by the way. And what it's causing is for people to sort of take a step back and say, 'Whoa, I still want the radiologist to look at the results, I still want the lawyer to review the documents.' This is a lot of change.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: You know who has the healthiest relationship here? Not a surprise, the doctor. But no, we had a health event. Yes we did. And what you see with the doctors is they're like, because their industry and their training and their whole philosophy is that there are certain bright lines that you never can cross. Yeah. They're like, 'Yeah, this thing helps me summarize notes, I review the notes, of course I review the notes.' You know, and they're like, 'Of course medical students are going to abuse this technology, so I'm going to have to stay on them and hit them with a stick every time I see it.' Like they have already factored in all of the abuses because they actually think that way. They're like, 'Oh, a new technology. We have to put it on rigid parameters to hue to the philosophy of medicine.'

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: that allows our culture to move forward at all costs.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: There's another reason which is by running to these tools you're not giving people an opportunity to build intuition to build skills that really come from you know a good doctor in about eight minutes can look at the whole picture and give you a read and that's experience right?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You just get spoiled in New York because you know my GP has probably seen 50,000 people.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It's a high volume play.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah exactly. So I walk in and he's like 'yep' like it's just like within about a minute. Okay so wait wait so I want to get back to the actual pricing, right? But there's a real thing going on which is that a lot of the things that we charged for with code were almost like syntactic like set up the system, install the database, put the right stuff on. That was thousands and thousands of dollars to do that well and that's pretty easy now.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Architecture remains really hard.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: An LLM can advise you about how a system should work.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It can even build that up, it's often not going to be right or purpose built for your actual needs. It's where software becomes human and so you still need that intentionality in the mix and then this is not me going like 'there's something wonderful about people that can never be replaced' that's not what I'm saying. I'm just saying like that is the actual product that most people want.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So okay so first of all is it a good idea to share artifacts like this with people? You assume people want to see as much value as they can right away and I love showing off and feeling like I'm a very smart boy.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Mhm, mhm.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: But is that a mistake? Should I just be like 'no come on in we'll whiteboard let's have a conversation'?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: No, it's not a mistake. I'll tell you why it's not a mistake. It feels like a mistake today and it won't feel like a mistake in two years. We went from, you know, there was a day where commercial flight was all about like they would wheel out a ham and it was first class and it was expensive to fly.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Spiral cut.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Sometimes the whole pig.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: They'd walk it down you could pet it.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It wasn't casual. Yeah there was a day when it wasn't casual to fly from New York to Chicago. And then a lot of time passed and then it became like taking a bus like it's nothing anymore right?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You go to LaGuardia.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Imagine one day it's first class and very expensive and the next day anybody can fly. The shift is too fast and that's why this person is so scrambled right? There's going to come a day where 'hey why didn't you show me something' is going to be the norm.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It's just too soon.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You know the other thing too. I'm going to bet you everyone who flew big propeller planes was like 'this is the apex technology'.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Oh of course.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You're never going to get faster and better and I am so good at keeping these propellers going.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Absolutely. And then jets show up and they're like 'nobody wants that it's loud, it's fast'. Yeah, yeah. All of it. And then they got cheaper and commoditized commercial flight became a thing and anybody could fly, which is wonderful. I just don't think people are like their brains haven't adjusted yet. Showing fully baked software in three days is still exploding people's minds.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Well it's also because it's...

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: has to be disposable. You haven't actually put the needs of the business into the software.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: That's confusing too.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so it's like I think seeing the artifact where it's it's literally like hey we already made the Thanksgiving turkey like really in like five minutes and now we're going to figure out what we're going to have for Thanksgiving.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: That is the conversation. Imagine saying that to your grandmother.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Right? and she's like, 'What the hell you talking about? Go to the store and get me cranberry sauce.'

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Alright, so here we are, architecture matters. We're going to end up communicating and showing people really big complicated artifacts in ways that maybe we didn't before.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yep.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: You want some tactical advice Paul? I could bill you by the hour. I could be and I could be like my hour is really really expensive because I have 20 years of experience and I'm going to bring it to bear on your problem and you need to pay me a million dollars an hour. Okay, so I could say that.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You could.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I could also say it's going to be a thousand dollars a month for the rest of your life but I'll take care of it for you. And I'm seeing you know I read an article today about a lawyer who's using Claude to run their practice and they've started to offer a subscription service.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: They're nearby to help with whatever.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah. I'll do all the contracts for you because I have this process that accelerates and isn't expensive for me so that's them driving the value but saying like I will I will take ownership and responsibility as your lawyer for these artifacts but I'm not going to try to hide that I'm accelerating the process with Claude. Yes. Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I'm going to imagine this lawyer is relatively young.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Well we don't have to imagine. We looked at his profile. This guy named Zack Shapiro on LinkedIn.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: He... yes he is not someone that has been groomed by getting billable hour bonuses at one of the white shoe law firms.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And he runs his own law firm.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Exactly. Here's the universal advice. The economics that worked in the past are going to get challenged and if you think you're going to be able to hold onto them by creating fancier packaging and more ceremony and stalling because the way you measure value is through the time you spend or the number of people you deploy you're in for an awakening. It's... that ship has sailed. And so you need to let go of the pre... the previous set of assumptions that drove the economics and that's hard for even us because that's what we used to do.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: We're having this conversation we're trying to do it in public but...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Correct. So I mean the other thing here's what's tricky. You're going to say that and everybody's going to be like 'Yeah but nobody's ready right now so I am going to stall just a little bit because that's in the best interest of me and my organization.'

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Oh it's happening.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Here's the problem with that and this is the actual advice. When you do that you are not focusing on building infrastructure and resilience for what's coming next you're focused on stalling and you have a fantasy you're going to set up like an innovation group where you're with the AI team that's going to build the whole new business. Those people will be hit by sticks and then they will go work for a startup that cherishes them as opposed to delivering the future to you on your terms.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Either don't, you can stall, but then you might get, you might get punished later. Or you can lean in and not stall and you might lose a lot of near-term revenue. Every attempt in the middle I have seen in 30 years has failed completely.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: That's the thing, right? And but it is a major rewiring of how you think about what you sell.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Because you can't change culture in one room over to the side. And that's always the fantasy.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Correct. Yeah. So, do you think there are law firms that have like labs right now? That's gotta be the worst thing ever.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No, I think, I think what they're doing is they're repurposing their IT groups.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Oh, that's definitely what they're doing.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Going from legal IT to AI innovator?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: You know who I feel for? There are, I don't know them, but there are startups that are worth a lot of money, not startups, there are companies that are worth a lot of money that just sell Microsoft Word macros for the law. They're very complex, they do all sorts of things, they go out to the web, they do special redlining features.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I mean, WordPress templates were half the Brazilian economy.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It's a big, big deal, right? And so back to pricing, let's reset it for a second. Okay?

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You used to sell what was a highly valued rare commodity: skilled people's time.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I mean, our agency was essentially like an API to get access to a lot of, you know, actual complicated humans. The promise was we would deal with all the complicated human parts and you would get your thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: They're great.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: They're great. Well, also, people want 'em. People want humans. Our process, if we pitch to you, we talk about, here's how what it's like to work with us. And the first couple of weeks it's like this, and the next couple of weeks are like that, and then the next month is like this. So people hearing that we are going to put care and also going to build trust with them, you can have great tools. The thing that—

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I mean the thing is, what you threw over the wall—

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: You could get TurboTax, but you still have an accountant. Yes, exactly. And look, the thing is you threw a thing over the wall to make a point, but that thing was really this sort of shocking artifact that is not the result of you having a lot of time and conversations with that person.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No, it's not deep.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It's not just that it's not deep. Part of relationship building, even in the business context, is that you are signaling to them that you're starting to internalize their needs and their pain. Like, you're starting to transfer over and like, I am joining you on this journey, right? And when you throw like a, what looks like a fully baked thing over the wall, it's not that different than someone hearing you out for five minutes. Like, I got it. I'll be back in two weeks, see ya. And they don't talk to you.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No one wants that. Even if they get it right, no one wants that.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I think that's really key because what they need is the relationship so that—

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I think we can work out the strategy. I got to call you, also. I think, you know, we got most of it right, but there's a few things we didn't get right. And then we're saying to them, 'I hear you.'

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I mean, this is, you know, again, and this is not me defending myself, like this is just somebody I've known for a while and I'm kind of prototyping this with them.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Totally, I know that. I get that.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: But my initial instinct is like, yeah, you probably shouldn't do that.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: This applies to the law, this applies to other industries where, look, don't misread. What took ten hours will now take two. But the relationship doesn't go away.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It also goes both ways, where if a person calls me up and is like, 'Hey, it used to take ten hours, now it takes two. I'm going to give you $100. You do it for me?'

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I'm going to hang up the phone.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so this is, this is the crux of it. Because this is really about us and how we're going to make money, Paul, you and I.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It's good to finally have this conversation after four years of building this business.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes. So, we can't sell time. Billable time, the idea of billing your time is no more. Like, we have to come to terms with that.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Well, most people, like we identify ourselves kind of as a product company with a little bit of agency. So that was off the table to start, but now it's kind of back. Like, talk about that, it's a little tricky.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I will give you my time. I will give you my time in the spirit of this relationship.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: That's what I was afraid of.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: But you're, I'm not going to run the meter. Our goal is to put something in your hands that you value deeply. And we're going to work with you to get to that thing. Now, when you get to that thing, and the reason I use the word value is what we're shifting from is time-based or people-based pricing to value-based pricing. Now what is value-based pricing? Value-based pricing is in the luxury world, right? Has very little, essentially they move away from the raw materials and the labor it takes to make a watch, and they're like, 'Well, what will the industry bear? What will the, sorry, what will the marketplace bear?'

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I mean, the archetypal term is the Veblen good, right? Which is the Or Veblen good, which is, you know, the status signal.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Exactly. The, the perceived value in the market is the value is value-based. Like, so you price it against that. Even though it costs me $600 in parts and maybe $1,000 in labor, I'm at $1,600 and then I have marketing and all that.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Don't forget R&D and all that.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: R&D and all that. But I'm going to charge $10,000 for this watch. Yep. And I can do that. Not everybody can do that, to be very clear. It takes a long time to build a brand such that you can do it. But that's a luxury brand. And this is what's tricky about our world. A luxury brand, you're buying it for status and you want it on your wrist so you can go to the dinner party, right? Our value-based pricing is actually value. Meaning I am literally going to save you millions of dollars. So wait, let me just finish this thought. If I'm going to save you millions of dollars, even though it took me two weeks, don't you think you should factor in that I'm going to save you millions of dollars? What's it worth to you? And the truth is when we try to have that conversation, we still-

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: cause everybody's very confused.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: It's a confusing time. I mean, you know what I go back to, we used to talk about this we had this concept of the $15 Volvo.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And that is that's what this is. Nobody wants a Volvo for $15 because something's wrong with it and it's supposed to protect your children.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Correct.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: The Volvo is in the middle of the luxury there's the mass produced, you know, Camry.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Nothing against Toyota Camry.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Not even the Camry. Like like stuff that comes in a bag from China that you bought like you bought these headphones and they show up and they're in that like that soft bag and they're from a part of China you've never heard of before. That's the like true like boy, just industrial process comes off, goes to you, goodbye.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: On the other side, there is the like a tiny gnome has made a watch for you using magic spells.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Stick with headphones. There's German brands of headphones, the unboxing experience you would think, you know, the Lord was coming out of this box, and it's thousands of dollars.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And I think in the middle there's the thing where it's like, yeah, I know I'm paying Apple is often in here where I'm like I know I'm paying a couple hundred extra.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: But I really don't want to deal with the other stuff.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I trust this brand, I trust what they do.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I when I buy Apple, because I buy a lot of Apple, I've got relatively good assurances of privacy, I have stability, I know there's continuity and in general like it's just not going to blow up my life in the way that Android and Windows is happy doing.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And so that's a premium for me, I, you know, that's at this stage of my life, I used to be an Android guy, at this stage of my life I'm just like whatever.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And I feel like that's the place that I most like exploring, which is sort of like, hey, it's going to be a little bit of a premium, but you're not going to have to think about it. We're going to take that part away for you.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And we're not going away.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah. So over here on the left you've got everybody who's like I can hack that myself in Clod code in an hour and a half. Okay? Over here on the right you have only I can solve this for you ever.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And in the middle which is I think the most interesting place on Earth is like you still need some custom stuff, but you can save a lot of money and time and it can run for 10 years for you because you don't want to think about software for the rest of your life. I'm going to do that for you.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Correct.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: So now how do you price that? Because you can't you over on the other you know it used to be T&M everywhere.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Well, I think two things. The good news for the buyer is that that incredibly expensive upfront implementation cost has plummeted. We can't justify it. If something takes me five weeks to get in your hands, right, I can't charge you as if it's 30 weeks. It just can't do it anymore.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: No. And that part of the process where it's like the product manager will write the PRD and then we'll build the prototype like really you're buying the relationship in a much more direct way than you used to buy a lot of artifacts along the way towards the relationship.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Correct.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Now you're more buying the relationship with software on the other side.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I think I think that is like such an interesting outcome of all the robots are taking over.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Well that's lawyers, that's lawyers, that's actually accountants, that's like...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Relationship matters a lot, right? And so, okay, so then how do I make money, right? I can't charge you like I used to charge you because the thing took me five weeks. So here's, first off, I have to come to terms with the fact that my margins may still be good even though it took five weeks. So the thing that you held precious as worth a half a million dollars might just be worth 100 grand. And you just need to accept that. Because it didn't cost you that much to make it.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You're going to do more of it.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: You're going to do more of it.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And then you'll make it up in volume, literally.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Exactly. Exactly. So come to terms with it, right? And it's okay. And this is my point about if you were doing 20 years of billings at a law firm, you're not going to be as forward-looking because boy, the gravy train was good.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: You know, it's funny though, is in like the software industry has always said that this is the future, but we liked it when we commoditized things into products and then you could sell a lot of the product and that person would get really rich. Yeah. And that's kind of how we started Aboard. We're like, 'Oh, we were doing an agency. Let's do all our agency stuff as a product. And we'll sell it. And we're going to be really awesome,' right? And then AI shows up and it's like, 'Nah, it's kind of in the middle now.'

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It's in the middle. I think that's the message here, which is, okay, so I don't get to charge as much. So wait, can I make enough money? Is there enough volume of opportunity? I don't know. But there is another path that's opened up on the other side of this is that when you're working at this velocity and delivering that quickly, the customer, whether you're a lawyer or whether you're a buyer of software, doesn't want you to leave. And so there is a new dynamic that's taking hold. Our friend at the law firm has a subscription model.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: There are old men in the big firms looking at that and snickering.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Oh, yeah, they think that's the dumbest thing they've ever heard.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Dumbest thing of- that is the future.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, good luck to you, buddy, with your subscription model.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: That is the future. Do you know why that's the future? Because he can probably assign 100 subscriptions to two people.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I think that's right. Because the- the tools are incredible, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Well, and what's going to happen is the deep expert. So I'm thinking of people in our firm. The product manager who is now a solution engineer. Everybody's a solution engineer. But the product manager is now able to ship what used to be required partnership with engineering. They can build a pretty robust business application using our platform. They put in the words, they get the stuff, they tweak the database a little bit using the interface, and now you can give them your data and off we go. Okay, so we're having a lot of those conversations. The engineer can also build that in like 10 minutes.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: But the engineer can go off and do really ugly database stuff that used to take an enormous amount of time. They can do 10 times more of it.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Sure.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: They can be like, 'Oh, you know what? I wanted a vector database here, but it was always a chore to set up and it was going to be too expensive. Now I snap my fingers and I got it.'

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yep.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: So the engineer can be going really, really deep. The product manager can be shipping. Those worlds can line up. But- and that I think is the new middle.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And what is a buyer paying for? I'm not going to-

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: ...be orthodox about exactly what they're paying for. We have clients today that frankly want to know that

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Customers.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Customers, that we have a dedicated team committed to them.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And that they just want that. And that's okay. That you can be have a successful business.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I want that. I don't want to call my lawyer and then I get an answering machine with somebody else's voice from a different firm.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Exactly. And we have others that don't want to pay that upfront, and they don't have to, but they are going to pay something akin to our licensing plus people model over time. So look, the good news here is that

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Anything over like three, 400 bucks a month

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: people would like to talk to somebody.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Always. Always. You just... you kind of need to, because otherwise you're getting fleeced.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: That's right.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It's not easy. Look, we're not young upstarts. Like I said, we had an old way of billing and so we are having to reboot it. And that's just life. Reboot it. Like, the law firms... the law firms are going to move slow. The big consulting firms, we talk about big consulting... just live... they live and breathe for billable hours are going... are going to be slow. Why? Because it's not in their best interest to move fast. And the truth is the world doesn't move that quickly. We're moving quickly because we're a startup and we get to talk about this stuff and we don't have to answer to anyone. But go talk to an organization that's over 500 people, all of this talk is going to move... all this is going to move real slow.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: There's two signals. One is are they giving everyone really good high value access to expensive LLMs? Like, you know, the premier package?

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It's a great point.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I mean, like Accenture gave 30,000 people Claude Code. I'm like, alright, that's good. Step one. That's good. Good news for Accenture. If they're saying, 'we've got an AI innovation group that's doing amazing stuff and it's they're going to ship a couple little products but then they're they're hoping it stays away from the rest of the org,' I would... I would mark that stock down a little bit. That's not... that's not going to work.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: 100 percent. You have to be willing to allow these changes to insinuate themselves into your culture, into your social dynamics and power dynamics. People are going to crush a lot of dreams with this stuff. There are old men and old women who are SVPs of this and that that are going to be like, 'get out of here with that.'

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Thank you everybody for listening. This has been the Aboard podcast.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Now if you are an SVP

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And you want to innovate

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Get in touch. Because

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And you don't want to shatter your culture.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, that's right. You should reach out to us because we have amazing people and amazing tools that ship production grade software.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Honestly, why you should call us is you want to gently shatter your culture.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Yeah, exactly. That's what we're for. Alright, so get in touch. Hello@aboard.com. Check out aboard.com, a lovely website made for everyone. Give us five stars, give us those thumbs up. We cherish it. We welcome it.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: We love feedback, good and bad. We want to know about all of it. And honestly, ask questions. We love that too. It's great.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: And just last... last point, Paul, all you old men and women are aging very gracefully. I just want to say that as we pitch our own product to them.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: And I'm just not on the bike right now, so I'm not...

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: I think we're turning, we're turning a corner.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I'm not aging gracefully the last couple of months.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: It's been a rough couple of months.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I know. I'm looking forward to that.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: I know, but it's like... it's been nothing but snow.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Alright, let's get out there.

full_transcript.speaker: Paul Ford
full_transcript.text: Have a great week.

full_transcript.speaker: Rich Ziade
full_transcript.text: Bye!

highlights: AI tools have dramatically reduced the cost and time required to build software prototypes, turning what once took months and hundreds of thousands of dollars into a process that can be completed in hours for a few dollars.

Delivering a finished product too quickly can paradoxically damage client trust, as business relationships often depend on the gradual signaling that a provider has internalized a client’s specific needs and pain points.

The traditional billable hour model is becoming obsolete because AI allows experts to work at such high speeds that they must shift toward value-based pricing, charging for the actual impact and savings delivered rather than time logged.

A "new middle" is emerging in the software industry where the primary value is no longer just the code, but the human relationship and the accountability that ensures a product is purpose-built and maintained.

Organizations that isolate AI into small innovation labs instead of allowing it to rewire their core culture and power dynamics risk being outcompeted by firms that fully integrate these efficiencies into their workflow.
summary:
In this episode of the *Aboard* podcast, hosts Paul Ford and Rich Ziade explore how AI is fundamentally disrupting the economics of software development and professional services. Paul shares an anecdote about "vibe-coding" a functional data-explorer prototype for a potential client in just two hours using Claude Vision—a project that would have traditionally required months of work and a $400,000 budget. This massive compression of effort highlights a growing tension in the industry: as the technical "scaffolding" of software becomes commoditized and nearly instantaneous, the traditional model of charging for billable hours and labor is becoming obsolete.

The discussion shifts toward the necessity of value-based pricing and the enduring importance of human relationships. The hosts argue that while AI can generate high-quality artifacts quickly, throwing a "fully baked" product over the wall too soon can actually alienate clients by skipping the trust-building phase. They suggest that the future of professional services—including law and consulting—lies in a subscription-based "middle ground" where clients pay for deep expertise, reliability, and an ongoing partnership rather than just the time spent on a task. 

Ultimately, the overarching theme is the urgent need for organizational "rewiring" in the face of AI. The hosts warn that established firms—such as "white shoe" law firms or large consulting groups—are incentivized to stall this transition to protect their existing margins. However, they emphasize that true resilience will come from integrating AI into an organization’s core culture and power dynamics rather than isolating it in "innovation labs." As AI handles the syntactic and repetitive work, the human element becomes a premium luxury focused on intentionality, intuition, and long-term strategy.
description:
With AI drastically cutting delivery times in tech and beyond, how should practitioners price their time? On this week’s podcast, Paul tells Rich about a recent experience with a potential client, where he skipped steps and rapidly vibe-coded through the prototyping process and they….didn’t really know what to make of the result. If things that used to take months can now be done in hours, what are clients actually paying for? 

Captions are auto-generated; for audio, show notes, and a full transcript, visit our website: https://aboard.com/podcast/expertise-matters-more-than-ever/
topics: The shift from billable hours to value-based pricing in professional services
The impact of AI tools on the speed and cost of software prototyping
Client reactions and trust issues related to rapid AI-driven delivery
The evolving roles of product managers and engineers in the age of AI
How professional industries like law and medicine are adapting to AI technology
